documents.content: In Foundation Year English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that English is one of many languages spoken in Australia and that different languages may be spoken by family, classmates and community
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Explore how language is used differently at home and school depending on the relationships between people
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that language can be used to explore ways of expressing needs, likes and dislikes

In Foundation Year English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that texts can take many forms, can be very short (for example an exit sign) or quite long (for example an information book or a film) and that stories and informative texts have different purposes
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that some language in written texts is unlike everyday spoken language

In Foundation Year English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that punctuation is a feature of written text different from letters; recognise how capital letters are used for names, and that capital letters and full stops signal the beginning and end of sentences

In Foundation Year English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand concepts about print and screen, including how books, film and simple digital texts work, and know some features of print, for example directionality
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Recognise that sentences are key units for expressing ideas
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Recognise that texts are made up of words and groups of words that make meaning
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Explore the different contribution of words and images to meaning in stories and informative texts

In Foundation Year English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand the use of vocabulary in familiar contexts related to everyday experiences, personal interests and topics taught at school

In Foundation Year English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Recognise and generate rhyming words, alliteration patterns, syllables and sounds (phonemes) in spoken words

In Foundation Year English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Recognise and name all upper and lower case letters (graphemes) and know the most common sound that each letter represents
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use knowledge of letters and sounds including onset and rime to spell words
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Know how to read and write some high-frequency words and other familiar words
In Foundation Year English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand that words are units of meaning and can be made of more than one meaningful part

In Foundation Year English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Segment sentences into individual words and orally blend and segment onset and rime in single syllable spoken words, and isolate, blend and manipulate phonemes in single syllable words

In Foundation Year English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Write consonant-vowel-consonant (CVC) words by representing some sounds with the appropriate letters, and blend sounds associated with letters when reading CVC words

In Foundation Year English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Recognise that texts are created by authors who tell stories and share experiences that may be similar or different to students’ own experiences
In Foundation Year English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Respond to texts, identifying favourite stories, authors and illustrators
In Foundation Year English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Share feelings and thoughts about the events and characters in texts
In Foundation Year English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Identify some features of texts including events and characters and retell events from a text

In Foundation Year English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Recognise some different types of literary texts and identify some characteristic features of literary texts, for example beginnings and endings of traditional texts and rhyme in poetry
In Foundation Year English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Replicate the rhythms and sound patterns in stories, rhymes, songs and poems from a range of cultures
In Foundation Year English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Retell familiar literary texts through performance, use of illustrations and images
In Foundation Year English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Innovate on familiar texts through play
In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Identify some familiar texts and the contexts in which they are used

In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Listen to and respond orally to texts and to the communication of others in informal and structured classroom situations

In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills including listening while others speak, using appropriate voice levels, articulation and body language, gestures and eye contact
In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Deliver short oral presentations to peers
In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Identify some differences between imaginative and informative texts

In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Read decodable and predictable texts, practising phrasing and fluency, and monitor meaning using concepts about print and emerging contextual, semantic, grammatical and phonic knowledge

In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to understand and discuss texts listened to, viewed or read independently
In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Create short texts to explore, record and report ideas and events using familiar words and beginning writing knowledge
In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Participate in shared editing of students’ own texts for meaning, spelling, capital letters and full stops
In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Produce some lower case and upper case letters using learned letter formations
In Foundation Year English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Construct texts using software including word processing programs
In Year 1 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Discuss how authors create characters using language and images

In Year 1 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Discuss characters and events in a range of literary texts and share personal responses to these texts, making connections with students' own experiences
In Year 1 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Express preferences for specific texts and authors and listen to the opinions of others

In Year 1 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Discuss features of plot, character and setting in different types of literature and explore some features of characters in different texts

In Year 1 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Listen to, recite and perform poems, chants, rhymes and songs, imitating and inventing sound patterns including alliteration and rhyme
In Year 1 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Recreate texts imaginatively using drawing, writing, performance and digital forms of communication
In Year 1 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Innovate on familiar texts by using similar characters, repetitive patterns or vocabulary
In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Respond to texts drawn from a range of cultures and experiences

In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Engage in conversations and discussions, using active listening behaviours, showing interest, and contributing ideas, information and questions

In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills including turn-taking, recognising the contributions of others, speaking clearly and using appropriate volume and pace
In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Make short presentations using some introduced text structures and language, for example opening statements
In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Describe some differences between imaginative informative and persuasive texts

In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Read decodable and predictable texts using developing phrasing, fluency, contextual, semantic, grammatical and phonic knowledge and emerging text processing strategies, for example prediction, monitoring meaning and re-reading

In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to build literal and inferred meaning about key events, ideas and information in texts that they listen to, view and read by drawing on growing knowledge of context, text structures and language features

In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Create short imaginative and informative texts that show emerging use of appropriate text structure, sentence-level grammar, word choice, spelling, punctuation and appropriate multimodal elements, for example illustrations and diagrams
In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Re-read student’s own texts and discuss possible changes to improve meaning, spelling and punctuation
In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Write using unjoined lower case and upper case letters
In Year 1 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Construct texts that incorporate supporting images using software including word processing programs

In Year 1 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that people use different systems of communication to cater to different needs and purposes and that many people may use sign systems to communicate with others

In Year 1 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that language is used in combination with other means of communication, for example facial expressions and gestures to interact with others
In Year 1 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that there are different ways of asking for information, making offers and giving commands
In Year 1 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Explore different ways of expressing emotions, including verbal, visual, body language and facial expressions
In Year 1 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that the purposes texts serve shape their structure in predictable ways
In Year 1 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand patterns of repetition and contrast in simple texts

In Year 1 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Recognise that different types of punctuation, including full stops, question marks and exclamation marks, signal sentences that make statements, ask questions, express emotion or give commands

In Year 1 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand concepts about print and screen, including how different types of texts are organised using page numbering, tables of content, headings and titles, navigation buttons, bars and links

In Year 1 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Identify the parts of a simple sentence that represent ‘What’s happening?’, ‘What state is being described?’, ‘Who or what is involved?’ and the surrounding circumstances

In Year 1 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Explore differences in words that represent people, places and things (nouns, including pronouns), happenings and states (verbs), qualities (adjectives) and details such as when, where and how (adverbs)
In Year 1 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Compare different kinds of images in narrative and informative texts and discuss how they contribute to meaning

In Year 1 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand the use of vocabulary in everyday contexts as well as a growing number of school contexts, including appropriate use of formal and informal terms of address in different contexts

In Year 1 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Manipulate phonemes in spoken words by addition, deletion and substitution of initial, medial and final phonemes to generate new words

In Year 1 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Use short vowels, common long vowels, consonant digraphs and consonant blends when writing, and blend these to read single syllable words
In Year 1 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand that a letter can represent more than one sound and that a syllable must contain a vowel sound
In Year 1 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to spell one and two syllable words with common letter patterns
In Year 1 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Recognise and know how to use simple grammatical morphemes to create word families
In Year 1 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Use visual memory to read and write high-frequency words
In Year 1 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Segment consonant blends or clusters into separate phonemes at the beginnings and ends of one syllable words
In Year 2 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Discuss how depictions of characters in print, sound and images reflect the contexts in which they were created
In Year 2 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Compare opinions about characters, events and settings in and between texts
In Year 2 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Identify aspects of different types of literary texts that entertain, and give reasons for personal preferences

In Year 2 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Discuss the characters and settings of different texts and explore how language is used to present these features in different ways
In Year 2 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Identify, reproduce and experiment with rhythmic, sound and word patterns in poems, chants, rhymes and songs
In Year 2 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create events and characters using different media that develop key events and characters from literary texts
In Year 2 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Innovate on familiar texts by experimenting with character, setting or plot

In Year 2 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that spoken, visual and written forms of language are different modes of communication with different features and their use varies according to the audience, purpose, context and cultural background

In Year 2 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that language varies when people take on different roles in social and classroom interactions and how the use of key interpersonal language resources varies depending on context
In Year 2 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Identify language that can be used for appreciating texts and the qualities of people and things

In Year 2 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that different types of texts have identifiable text structures and language features that help the text serve its purpose

In Year 2 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how texts are made cohesive through language features, including word associations, synonyms, and antonyms
In Year 2 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Recognise that capital letters signal proper nouns and commas are used to separate items in lists

In Year 2 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Know some features of text organisation including page and screen layouts, alphabetical order, and different types of diagrams, for example timelines

In Year 2 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand that simple connections can be made between ideas by using a compound sentence with two or more clauses usually linked by a coordinating conjunction

In Year 2 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand that nouns represent people, places, concrete objects and abstract concepts; that there are three types of nouns: common, proper and pronouns; and that noun groups/phrases can be expanded using articles and adjectives

In Year 2 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Identify visual representations of characters’ actions, reactions, speech and thought processes in narratives, and consider how these images add to or contradict or multiply the meaning of accompanying words

In Year 2 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand the use of vocabulary about familiar and new topics and experiment with and begin to make conscious choices of vocabulary to suit audience and purpose

In Year 2 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Orally manipulate more complex sounds in spoken words through knowledge of blending and segmenting sounds, phoneme deletion and substitution in combination with use of letters in reading and writing

In Year 2 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use knowledge of digraphs, long vowels, blends and silent letters to spell one and two syllable words including some compound words
In Year 2 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Build morphemic word families using knowledge of prefixes and suffixes

In Year 2 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Use knowledge of letter patterns and morphemes to read and write high-frequency words and words whose spelling is not predictable from their sounds

In Year 2 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Use most letter-sound matches including vowel digraphs, less common long vowel patterns, letter clusters and silent letters when reading and writing words of one or more syllable
In Year 2 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand that a sound can be represented by various letter combinations
In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Discuss different texts on a similar topic, identifying similarities and differences between the texts

In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Listen for specific purposes and information, including instructions, and extend students’ own and others' ideas in discussions

In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills including initiating topics, making positive statements and voicing disagreement in an appropriate manner, speaking clearly and varying tone, volume and pace appropriately
In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Rehearse and deliver short presentations on familiar and new topics
In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Identify the audience of imaginative, informative and persuasive texts

In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Read less predictable texts with phrasing and fluency by combining contextual, semantic, grammatical and phonic knowledge using text processing strategies, for example monitoring meaning, predicting, rereading and self-correcting

In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to build literal and inferred meaning and begin to analyse texts by drawing on growing knowledge of context, language and visual features and print and multimodal text structures

In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Create short imaginative, informative and persuasive texts using growing knowledge of text structures and language features for familiar and some less familiar audiences, selecting print and multimodal elements appropriate to the audience and purpose
In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Re-read and edit text for spelling, sentence-boundary punctuation and text structure
In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Write legibly and with growing fluency using unjoined upper case and lower case letters
In Year 2 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Construct texts featuring print, visual and audio elements using software, including word processing programs

In Year 3 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Discuss texts in which characters, events and settings are portrayed in different ways, and speculate on the authors’ reasons
In Year 3 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Draw connections between personal experiences and the worlds of texts, and share responses with others
In Year 3 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Develop criteria for establishing personal preferences for literature

In Year 3 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Discuss how language is used to describe the settings in texts, and explore how the settings shape the events and influence the mood of the narrative

In Year 3 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Discuss the nature and effects of some language devices used to enhance meaning and shape the reader’s reaction, including rhythm and onomatopoeia in poetry and prose

In Year 3 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create imaginative texts based on characters, settings and events from students’ own and other cultures using visual features, for example perspective, distance and angle

In Year 3 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create texts that adapt language features and patterns encountered in literary texts, for example characterisation, rhyme, rhythm, mood, music, sound effects and dialogue
In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Identify the point of view in a text and suggest alternative points of view

In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Listen to and contribute to conversations and discussions to share information and ideas and negotiate in collaborative situations

In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills, including active listening behaviours and communicate in a clear, coherent manner using a variety of everyday and learned vocabulary and appropriate tone, pace, pitch and volume
In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Plan and deliver short presentations, providing some key details in logical sequence
In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Identify the audience and purpose of imaginative, informative and persuasive texts

In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Read an increasing range of different types of texts by combining contextual, semantic, grammatical and phonic knowledge, using text processing strategies, for example monitoring, predicting, confirming, rereading, reading on and self-correcting

In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to build literal and inferred meaning and begin to evaluate texts by drawing on a growing knowledge of context, text structures and language features

In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Plan, draft and publish imaginative, informative and persuasive texts demonstrating increasing control over text structures and language features and selecting print,and multimodal elements appropriate to the audience and purpose
In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Re-read and edit texts for meaning, appropriate structure, grammatical choices and punctuation
In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Write using joined letters that are clearly formed and consistent in size

In Year 3 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Use software including word processing programs with growing speed and efficiency to construct and edit texts featuring visual, print and audio elements

In Year 3 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that languages have different written and visual communication systems, different oral traditions and different ways of constructing meaning

In Year 3 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that successful cooperation with others depends on shared use of social conventions, including turn-taking patterns, and forms of address that vary according to the degree of formality in social situations
In Year 3 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Examine how evaluative language can be varied to be more or less forceful

In Year 3 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how different types of texts vary in use of language choices, depending on their purpose and context (for example, tense and types of sentences)
In Year 3 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that paragraphs are a key organisational feature of written texts

In Year 3 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Know that word contractions are a feature of informal language and that apostrophes of contraction are used to signal missing letters
In Year 3 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Identify the features of online texts that enhance navigation

In Year 3 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand that a clause is a unit of grammar usually containing a subject and a verb and that these need to be in agreement

In Year 3 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand that verbs represent different processes, for example doing, thinking, saying, and relating and that these processes are anchored in time through tense

In Year 3 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Identify the effect on audiences of techniques, for example shot size, vertical camera angle and layout in picture books, advertisements and film segments
In Year 3 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Learn extended and technical vocabulary and ways of expressing opinion including modal verbs and adverbs
In Year 3 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use letter-sound relationships and less common letter patterns to spell words
In Year 3 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Recognise and know how to write most high frequency words including some homophones

In Year 3 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to apply knowledge of letter-sound relationships, syllables, and blending and segmenting to fluently read and write multisyllabic words with more complex letter patterns
In Year 3 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Know how to use common prefixes and suffixes, and generalisations for adding a suffix to a base word
In Year 4 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Make connections between the ways different authors may represent similar storylines, ideas and relationships
In Year 4 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Discuss literary experiences with others, sharing responses and expressing a point of view
In Year 4 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Use metalanguage to describe the effects of ideas, text structures and language features of literary texts

In Year 4 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Discuss how authors and illustrators make stories exciting, moving and absorbing and hold readers’ interest by using various techniques, for example character development and plot tension

In Year 4 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Understand, interpret and experiment with a range of devices and deliberate word play in poetry and other literary texts, for example nonsense words, spoonerisms, neologisms and puns
In Year 4 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts that explore students’ own experiences and imagining
In Year 4 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts by developing storylines, characters and settings

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Identify and explain language features of texts from earlier times and compare with the vocabulary, images, layout and content of contemporary texts

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Interpret ideas and information in spoken texts and listen for key points in order to carry out tasks and use information to share and extend ideas and information

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills such as acknowledging another’s point of view and linking students’ response to the topic, using familiar and new vocabulary and a range of vocal effects such as tone, pace, pitch and volume to speak clearly and coherently

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Plan, rehearse and deliver presentations incorporating learned content and taking into account the particular purposes and audiences

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Identify characteristic features used in imaginative, informative and persuasive texts to meet the purpose of the text

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Read different types of texts by combining contextual , semantic, grammatical and phonic knowledge using text processing strategies for example monitoring meaning, cross checking and reviewing

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to build literal and inferred meaning to expand content knowledge, integrating and linking ideas and analysing and evaluating texts

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Plan, draft and publish imaginative, informative and persuasive texts containing key information and supporting details for a widening range of audiences, demonstrating increasing control over text structures and language features
In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Re-read and edit for meaning by adding, deleting or moving words or word groups to improve content and structure
In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Write using clearly-formed joined letters, and develop increased fluency and automaticity

In Year 4 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Use a range of software including word processing programs to construct, edit and publish written text, and select, edit and place visual, print and audio elements

In Year 4 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that Standard Australian English is one of many social dialects used in Australia, and that while it originated in England it has been influenced by many other languages

In Year 4 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that social interactions influence the way people engage with ideas and respond to others for example when exploring and clarifying the ideas of others, summarising their own views and reporting them to a larger group
In Year 4 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand differences between the language of opinion and feeling and the language of factual reporting or recording

In Year 4 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how texts vary in complexity and technicality depending on the approach to the topic, the purpose and the intended audience

In Year 4 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how texts are made cohesive through the use of linking devices including pronoun reference and text connectives
In Year 4 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Recognise how quotation marks are used in texts to signal dialogue, titles and quoted (direct) speech

In Year 4 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Identify features of online texts that enhance readability including text, navigation, links, graphics and layout

In Year 4 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand that the meaning of sentences can be enriched through the use of noun groups/phrases and verb groups/phrases and prepositional phrases
In Year 4 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Investigate how quoted (direct) and reported (indirect) speech work in different types of text

In Year 4 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how adverb groups/phrases and prepositional phrases work in different ways to provide circumstantial details about an activity

In Year 4 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Explore the effect of choices when framing an image, placement of elements in the image, and salience on composition of still and moving images in a range of types of texts

In Year 4 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Incorporate new vocabulary from a range of sources into students’ own texts including vocabulary encountered in research

In Year 4 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use knowledge of letter patterns including double letters, spelling generalisations, morphemic word families, common prefixes and suffixes and word origins to spell more complex words

In Year 4 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Read and write a large core of high frequency words including homophones and know how to use context to identify correct spelling

In Year 4 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use phonic knowledge to read and write multisyllabic words with more complex letter combinations, including a variety of vowel sounds and known prefixes and suffixes

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Show how ideas and points of view in texts are conveyed through the use of vocabulary, including idiomatic expressions, objective and subjective language, and that these can change according to context

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Clarify understanding of content as it unfolds in formal and informal situations, connecting ideas to students’ own experiences and present and justify a point of view

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills, for example paraphrasing, questioning and interpreting non-verbal cues and choose vocabulary and vocal effects appropriate for different audiences and purposes

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Plan, rehearse and deliver presentations for defined audiences and purposes incorporating accurate and sequenced content and multimodal elements

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Identify and explain characteristic text structures and language features used in imaginative, informative and persuasive texts to meet the purpose of the text

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Navigate and read texts for specific purposes applying appropriate text processing strategies, for example predicting and confirming, monitoring meaning, skimming and scanning

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to analyse information, integrating and linking ideas from a variety of print and digital sources

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Plan, draft and publish imaginative, informative and persuasive print and multimodal texts, choosing text structures, language features, images and sound appropriate to purpose and audience
In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Re-read and edit student’s own and others’ work using agreed criteria for text structures and language features
In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Develop a handwriting style that is becoming legible, fluent and automatic

In Year 5 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Use a range of software including word processing programs with fluency to construct, edit and publish written text, and select, edit and place visual, print and audio elements

In Year 5 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Identify aspects of literary texts that convey details or information about particular social, cultural and historical contexts

In Year 5 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Present a point of view about particular literary texts using appropriate metalanguage, and reflecting on the viewpoints of others
In Year 5 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Use metalanguage to describe the effects of ideas, text structures and language features on particular audiences

In Year 5 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Recognise that ideas in literary texts can be conveyed from different viewpoints, which can lead to different kinds of interpretations and responses

In Year 5 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Understand, interpret and experiment with sound devices and imagery, including simile, metaphor and personification, in narratives, shape poetry, songs, anthems and odes

In Year 5 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts using realistic and fantasy settings and characters that draw on the worlds represented in texts students have experienced
In Year 5 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts that experiment with structures, ideas and stylistic features of selected authors
In Year 5 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that the pronunciation, spelling and meanings of words have histories and change over time

In Year 5 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that patterns of language interaction vary across social contexts and types of texts and that they help to signal social roles and relationships
In Year 5 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand how to move beyond making bare assertions and take account of differing perspectives and points of view
In Year 5 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how texts vary in purpose, structure and topic as well as the degree of formality

In Year 5 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that the starting point of a sentence gives prominence to the message in the text and allows for prediction of how the text will unfold

In Year 5 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how the grammatical category of possessives is signalled through apostrophes and how to use apostrophes with common and proper nouns

In Year 5 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Investigate how the organisation of texts into chapters, headings, subheadings, home pages and sub pages for online texts and according to chronology or topic can be used to predict content and assist navigation

In Year 5 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand the difference between main and subordinate clauses and that a complex sentence involves at least one subordinate clause

In Year 5 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how noun groups/phrases and adjective groups/phrases can be expanded in a variety of ways to provide a fuller description of the person, place, thing or idea

In Year 5 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Explain sequences of images in print texts and compare these to the ways hyperlinked digital texts are organised, explaining their effect on viewers’ interpretations

In Year 5 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand the use of vocabulary to express greater precision of meaning, and know that words can have different meanings in different contexts

In Year 5 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use knowledge of known words, base words, prefixes and suffixes, word origins, letter patterns and spelling generalisations to spell new words
In Year 5 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Explore less common plurals, and understand how a suffix changes the meaning or grammatical form of a word

In Year 5 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use phonic knowledge to read and write less familiar words that share common letter patterns but have different pronunciations

In Year 6 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Make connections between students’ own experiences and those of characters and events represented in texts drawn from different historical, social and cultural contexts
In Year 6 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Analyse and evaluate similarities and differences in texts on similar topics, themes or plots

In Year 6 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Identify and explain how choices in language, for example modality, emphasis, repetition and metaphor, influence personal response to different texts

In Year 6 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Identify, describe, and discuss similarities and differences between texts, including those by the same author or illustrator, and evaluate characteristics that define an author’s individual style

In Year 6 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Identify the relationship between words, sounds, imagery and language patterns in narratives and poetry such as ballads, limericks and free verse
In Year 6 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts that adapt or combine aspects of texts students have experienced in innovative ways

In Year 6 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Experiment with text structures and language features and their effects in creating literary texts, for example, using imagery, sentence variation, metaphor and word choice

In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Compare texts including media texts that represent ideas and events in different ways, explaining the effects of the different approaches

In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Participate in and contribute to discussions, clarifying and interrogating ideas, developing and supporting arguments, sharing and evaluating information, experiences and opinions

In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills, varying conventions of spoken interactions such as voice volume, tone, pitch and pace, according to group size, formality of interaction and needs and expertise of the audience

In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Plan, rehearse and deliver presentations, selecting and sequencing appropriate content and multimodal elements for defined audiences and purposes, making appropriate choices for modality and emphasis
In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Analyse how text structures and language features work together to meet the purpose of a text

In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Select, navigate and read texts for a range of purposes, applying appropriate text processing strategies and interpreting structural features, for example table of contents, glossary, chapters, headings and subheadings

In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to interpret and analyse information and ideas, comparing content from a variety of textual sources including media and digital texts
In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Analyse strategies authors use to influence readers

In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Plan, draft and publish imaginative, informative and persuasive texts, choosing and experimenting with text structures, language features, images and digital resources appropriate to purpose and audience
In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Re-read and edit students’ own and others’ work using agreed criteria and explaining editing choices
In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Develop a handwriting style that is legible, fluent and automatic and varies according to audience and purpose
In Year 6 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Use a range of software, including word processing programs, learning new functions as required to create texts

In Year 6 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that different social and geographical dialects or accents are used in Australia in addition to Standard Australian English

In Year 6 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that strategies for interaction become more complex and demanding as levels of formality and social distance increase
In Year 6 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand the uses of objective and subjective language and bias

In Year 6 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how authors often innovate on text structures and play with language features to achieve particular aesthetic, humorous and persuasive purposes and effects
In Year 6 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that cohesive links can be made in texts by omitting or replacing words
In Year 6 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand the uses of commas to separate clauses
In Year 6 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Investigate how complex sentences can be used in a variety of ways to elaborate, extend and explain ideas

In Year 6 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how ideas can be expanded and sharpened through careful choice of verbs, elaborated tenses and a range of adverb groups/phrases

In Year 6 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Identify and explain how analytical images like figures, tables, diagrams, maps and graphs contribute to our understanding of verbal information in factual and persuasive texts

In Year 6 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Investigate how vocabulary choices, including evaluative language can express shades of meaning, feeling and opinion

In Year 6 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use knowledge of known words, word origins including some Latin and Greek roots, base words, prefixes, suffixes, letter patterns and spelling generalisations to spell new words including technical words

In Year 6 English, under Language (Phonics and word knowledge), students learn to: Understand how to use phonic knowledge and accumulated understandings about blending, letter-sound relationships, common and uncommon letter patterns and phonic generalisations to read and write increasingly complex words
In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Analyse and explain the effect of technological innovations on texts, particularly media texts

In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Identify and discuss main ideas, concepts and points of view in spoken texts to evaluate qualities, for example the strength of an argument or the lyrical power of a poetic rendition

In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills when discussing and presenting ideas and information, selecting body language, voice qualities and other elements, (for example music and sound) to add interest and meaning

In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Plan, rehearse and deliver presentations, selecting and sequencing appropriate content and multimodal elements to promote a point of view or enable a new way of seeing

In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Analyse and explain the ways text structures and language features shape meaning and vary according to audience and purpose
In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use prior knowledge and text processing strategies to interpret a range of types of texts

In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to interpret, analyse and synthesise ideas and information, critiquing ideas and issues from a variety of textual sources

In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Compare the text structures and language features of multimodal texts, explaining how they combine to influence audiences

In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Plan, draft and publish imaginative, informative and persuasive texts, selecting aspects of subject matter and particular language, visual, and audio features to convey information and ideas
In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Edit for meaning by removing repetition, refining ideas, reordering sentences and adding or substituting words for impact
In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Consolidate a personal handwriting style that is legible, fluent and automatic and supports writing for extended periods

In Year 7 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Use a range of software, including word processing programs, to confidently create, edit and publish written and multimodal texts

In Year 7 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Identify and explore ideas and viewpoints about events, issues and characters represented in texts drawn from different historical, social and cultural contexts

In Year 7 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Reflect on ideas and opinions about characters, settings and events in literary texts, identifying areas of agreement and difference with others and justifying a point of view

In Year 7 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Compare the ways that language and images are used to create character, and to influence emotions and opinions in different types of texts
In Year 7 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Discuss aspects of texts, for example their aesthetic and social value, using relevant and appropriate metalanguage

In Year 7 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Recognise and analyse the ways that characterisation, events and settings are combined in narratives, and discuss the purposes and appeal of different approaches

In Year 7 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Understand, interpret and discuss how language is compressed to produce a dramatic effect in film or drama, and to create layers of meaning in poetry, for example haiku, tankas, couplets, free verse and verse novels

In Year 7 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts that adapt stylistic features encountered in other texts, for example, narrative viewpoint, structure of stanzas, contrast and juxtaposition

In Year 7 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Experiment with text structures and language features and their effects in creating literary texts, for example, using rhythm, sound effects, monologue, layout, navigation and colour

In Year 7 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand the way language evolves to reflect a changing world, particularly in response to the use of new technology for presenting texts and communicating
In Year 7 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand how accents, styles of speech and idioms express and create personal and social identities

In Year 7 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand how language is used to evaluate texts and how evaluations about a text can be substantiated by reference to the text and other sources

In Year 7 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand and explain how the text structures and language features of texts become more complex in informative and persuasive texts and identify underlying structures such as taxonomies, cause and effect, and extended metaphors

In Year 7 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that the coherence of more complex texts relies on devices that signal text structure and guide readers, for example overviews, initial and concluding paragraphs and topic sentences, indexes or site maps or breadcrumb trails for online texts

In Year 7 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand the use of punctuation to support meaning in complex sentences with prepositional phrases and embedded clauses

In Year 7 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Recognise and understand that subordinate clauses embedded within noun groups/phrases are a common feature of written sentence structures and increase the density of information

In Year 7 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how modality is achieved through discriminating choices in modal verbs, adverbs, adjectives and nouns

In Year 7 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Analyse how point of view is generated in visual texts by means of choices, for example gaze, angle and social distance

In Year 7 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Investigate vocabulary typical of extended and more academic texts and the role of abstract nouns, classification, description and generalisation in building specialised knowledge through language

In Year 7 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how to use spelling rules and word origins, for example Greek and Latin roots, base words, suffixes, prefixes, spelling patterns and generalisations to learn new words and how to spell them

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Analyse and explain how language has evolved over time and how technology and the media have influenced language use and forms of communication

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Interpret the stated and implied meanings in spoken texts, and use evidence to support or challenge different perspectives

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills for identified purposes, using voice and language conventions to suit different situations, selecting vocabulary, modulating voice and using elements such as music, images and sound for specific effects

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Plan, rehearse and deliver presentations, selecting and sequencing appropriate content, including multimodal elements, to reflect a diversity of viewpoints

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Analyse and evaluate the ways that text structures and language features vary according to the purpose of the text and the ways that referenced sources add authority to a text

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Apply increasing knowledge of vocabulary, text structures and language features to understand the content of texts

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to interpret and evaluate texts by reflecting on the validity of content and the credibility of sources, including finding evidence in the text for the author’s point of view

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Explore and explain the ways authors combine different modes and media in creating texts, and the impact of these choices on the viewer/listener

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Create imaginative, informative and persuasive texts that raise issues, report events and advance opinions, using deliberate language and textual choices, and including digital elements as appropriate

In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Experiment with text structures and language features to refine and clarify ideas to improve the effectiveness of students’ own texts
In Year 8 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Use a range of software, including word processing programs, to create, edit and publish texts imaginatively

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Explore the ways that ideas and viewpoints in literary texts drawn from different historical, social and cultural contexts may reflect or challenge the values of individuals and groups

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Explore the interconnectedness of Country/Place, People, Identity and Culture in texts including those by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors
In Year 8 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Share, reflect on, clarify and evaluate opinions and arguments about aspects of literary texts

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Understand and explain how combinations of words and images in texts are used to represent particular groups in society, and how texts position readers in relation to those groups

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Recognise and explain differing viewpoints about the world, cultures, individual people and concerns represented in texts

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Recognise, explain and analyse the ways literary texts draw on readers’ knowledge of other texts and enable new understanding and appreciation of aesthetic qualities

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Identify and evaluate devices that create tone, for example humour, wordplay, innuendo and parody in poetry, humorous prose, drama or visual texts

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Interpret and analyse language choices, including sentence patterns, dialogue, imagery and other language features, in short stories, literary essays and plays

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts that draw upon text structures and language features of other texts for particular purposes and effects

In Year 8 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Experiment with particular language features drawn from different types of texts, including combinations of language and visual choices to create new texts

In Year 8 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand the influence and impact that the English language has had on other languages or dialects and how English has been influenced in return
In Year 8 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand how conventions of speech adopted by communities influence the identities of people in those communities

In Year 8 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand how rhetorical devices are used to persuade and how different layers of meaning are developed through the use of metaphor, irony and parody

In Year 8 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Analyse how the text structures and language features of persuasive texts, including media texts, vary according to the medium and mode of communication

In Year 8 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how cohesion in texts is improved by strengthening the internal structure of paragraphs through the use of examples, quotations and substantiation of claims

In Year 8 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how coherence is created in complex texts through devices like lexical cohesion, ellipsis, grammatical theme and text connectives

In Year 8 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand the use of punctuation conventions, including colons, semicolons, dashes and brackets in formal and informal texts

In Year 8 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Analyse and examine how effective authors control and use a variety of clause structures, including clauses embedded within the structure of a noun group/phrase or clause
In Year 8 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand the effect of nominalisation in the writing of informative and persuasive texts

In Year 8 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Investigate how visual and multimodal texts allude to or draw on other texts or images to enhance and layer meaning
In Year 8 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Recognise that vocabulary choices contribute to the specificity, abstraction and style of texts

In Year 8 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how to apply learned knowledge consistently in order to spell accurately and to learn new words including nominalisations

In Year 9 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that Standard Australian English is a living language within which the creation and loss of words and the evolution of usage is ongoing
In Year 9 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that roles and relationships are developed and challenged through language and interpersonal skills

In Year 9 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Investigate how evaluation can be expressed directly and indirectly using devices, for example allusion, evocative vocabulary and metaphor
In Year 9 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand that authors innovate with text structures and language for specific purposes and effects

In Year 9 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Compare and contrast the use of cohesive devices in texts, focusing on how they serve to signpost ideas, to make connections and to build semantic associations between ideas

In Year 9 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how punctuation is used along with layout and font variations in constructing texts for different audiences and purposes
In Year 9 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Explain how authors creatively use the structures of sentences and clauses for particular effects
In Year 9 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how certain abstract nouns can be used to summarise preceding or subsequent stretches of text
In Year 9 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Analyse and explain the use of symbols, icons and myth in still and moving images and how these augment meaning
In Year 9 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Identify how vocabulary choices contribute to specificity, abstraction and stylistic effectiveness

In Year 9 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how spelling is used creatively in texts for particular effects, for example characterisation and humour and to represent accents and styles of speech

In Year 9 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Interpret and compare how representations of people and culture in literary texts are drawn from different historical, social and cultural contexts
In Year 9 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Present an argument about a literary text based on initial impressions and subsequent analysis of the whole text
In Year 9 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Reflect on, discuss and explore notions of literary value and how and why such notions vary according to context

In Year 9 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Explore and reflect on personal understanding of the world and significant human experience gained from interpreting various representations of life matters in texts

In Year 9 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Analyse texts from familiar and unfamiliar contexts, and discuss and evaluate their content and the appeal of an individual author’s literary style

In Year 9 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Investigate and experiment with the use and effect of extended metaphor, metonymy, allegory, icons, myths and symbolism in texts, for example poetry, short films, graphic novels, and plays on similar themes
In Year 9 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Analyse text structures and language features of literary texts, and make relevant comparisons with other texts

In Year 9 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts, including hybrid texts, that innovate on aspects of other texts, for example by using parody, allusion and appropriation

In Year 9 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Experiment with the ways that language features, image and sound can be adapted in literary texts, for example the effects of stereotypical characters and settings, the playfulness of humour and pun and the use of hyperlink

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Analyse how the construction and interpretation of texts, including media texts, can be influenced by cultural perspectives and other texts

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Listen to spoken texts constructed for different purposes, for example to entertain and to persuade, and analyse how language features of these texts position listeners to respond in particular ways

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use interaction skills to present and discuss an idea and to influence and engage an audience by selecting persuasive language, varying voice tone, pitch, and pace, and using elements such as music and sound effects

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Plan, rehearse and deliver presentations, selecting and sequencing appropriate content and multimodal elements for aesthetic and playful purposes

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Interpret, analyse and evaluate how different perspectives of issue, event, situation, individuals or groups are constructed to serve specific purposes in texts
In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Apply an expanding vocabulary to read increasingly complex texts with fluency and comprehension

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Use comprehension strategies to interpret and analyse texts, comparing and evaluating representations of an event, issue, situation or character in different texts

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Explore and explain the combinations of language and visual choices that authors make to present information, opinions and perspectives in different texts

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Create imaginative, informative and persuasive texts that present a point of view and advance or illustrate arguments, including texts that integrate visual, print and/or audio features

In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Review and edit students’ own and others’ texts to improve clarity and control over content, organisation, paragraphing, sentence structure, vocabulary and audio/visual features
In Year 9 English, under Literacy (Creating texts), students learn to: Use a range of software, including word processing programs, flexibly and imaginatively to publish texts

In Year 10 English, under Literature (Literature and context), students learn to: Compare and evaluate a range of representations of individuals and groups in different historical, social and cultural contexts
In Year 10 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Reflect on, extend, endorse or refute others’ interpretations of and responses to literature

In Year 10 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Analyse and explain how text structures, language features and visual features of texts and the context in which texts are experienced may influence audience response
In Year 10 English, under Literature (Responding to literature), students learn to: Evaluate the social, moral and ethical positions represented in texts

In Year 10 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Identify, explain and discuss how narrative viewpoint, structure, characterisation and devices including analogy and satire shape different interpretations and responses to a text

In Year 10 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Compare and evaluate how ‘voice’ as a literary device can be used in a range of different types of texts such as poetry to evoke particular emotional responses

In Year 10 English, under Literature (Examining literature), students learn to: Analyse and evaluate text structures and language features of literary texts and make relevant thematic and intertextual connections with other texts
In Year 10 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts that reflect an emerging sense of personal style and evaluate the effectiveness of these texts

In Year 10 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create literary texts with a sustained ‘voice’, selecting and adapting appropriate text structures, literary devices, language, auditory and visual structures and features for a specific purpose and intended audience
In Year 10 English, under Literature (Creating literature), students learn to: Create imaginative texts that make relevant thematic and intertextual connections with other texts

In Year 10 English, under Language (Language variation and change), students learn to: Understand that Standard Australian English in its spoken and written forms has a history of evolution and change and continues to evolve
In Year 10 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand how language use can have inclusive and exclusive social effects, and can empower or disempower people

In Year 10 English, under Language (Language for interaction), students learn to: Understand that people’s evaluations of texts are influenced by their value systems, the context and the purpose and mode of communication

In Year 10 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Compare the purposes, text structures and language features of traditional and contemporary texts in different media

In Year 10 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand how paragraphs and images can be arranged for different purposes, audiences, perspectives and stylistic effects
In Year 10 English, under Language (Text structure and organisation), students learn to: Understand conventions for citing others, and how to reference these in different ways

In Year 10 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Analyse and evaluate the effectiveness of a wide range of sentence and clause structures as authors design and craft texts

In Year 10 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Analyse how higher order concepts are developed in complex texts through language features including nominalisation, clause combinations, technicality and abstraction
In Year 10 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Evaluate the impact on audiences of different choices in the representation of still and moving images

In Year 10 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Refine vocabulary choices to discriminate between shades of meaning, with deliberate attention to the effect on audiences

In Year 10 English, under Language (Expressing and developing ideas), students learn to: Understand how to use knowledge of the spelling system to spell unusual and technical words accurately, for example those based on uncommon Greek and Latin roots

In Year 10 English, under Literacy (Texts in context), students learn to: Analyse and evaluate how people, cultures, places, events, objects and concepts are represented in texts, including media texts, through language, structural and/or visual choices

In Year 10 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Identify and explore the purposes and effects of different text structures and language features of spoken texts, and use this knowledge to create purposeful texts that inform, persuade and engage

In Year 10 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Use organisation patterns, voice and language conventions to present a point of view on a subject, speaking clearly, coherently and with effect, using logic, imagery and rhetorical devices to engage audiences

In Year 10 English, under Literacy (Interacting with others), students learn to: Plan, rehearse and deliver presentations, selecting and sequencing appropriate content and multimodal elements to influence a course of action

In Year 10 English, under Literacy (Interpreting, analysing, evaluating), students learn to: Identify and analyse implicit or explicit values, beliefs and assumptions in texts and how these are influenced by purposes and likely audiences
